message:
perf(stats,storage): defer stats config load + reorder label filters in list/count

Two related performance touches that share the same theme — pay
expensive work only when something downstream actually needs it.

== src/cli/commands/stats.rs: lazy config-layer load ==

`execute_inner()` was eagerly loading the project's full config
layer at the top of the function and stashing it into `config_layer`
before any of the stats computation began. The config layer is
moderately expensive to build (it merges N source files plus CLI
overrides), and only ONE downstream branch — the external-blockers
analysis — actually consults it. For projects with no external
dependencies (the common case), every `br stats` invocation paid
that cost for nothing.

Refactor to:

  - Capture the candidate `storage_ctx_for_config` (preloaded or
    owned) into a local at the top, but DO NOT call
    `load_config(...)`/`load_config_layer(...)` yet.
  - Declare `config_layer: Option<ConfigLayer> = None` and lazily
    populate it the first time a branch needs it.
  - The external-blockers branch now opens with:

        if config_layer.is_none() {
            config_layer = Some(load_stats_config_layer(...)?);
        }
        let external_db_paths = config::external_project_db_paths(
            config_layer.as_ref().expect("..."),
            beads_dir,
        );

    The `expect` is justified by the immediately-preceding
    `is_none()` populate; the message names the load context so a
    future regression that drops the populate fails loudly.
  - The use-color decision (which historically came from the eager
    load) moves into the same lazy path, keyed on whether output
    formatting actually needs color resolution.

Net effect: a `br stats` against a project without external
dependencies skips the config load entirely. Projects with
external deps pay the same cost as before.

== src/storage/sqlite.rs: relocate label-filter append ==

`SqliteStorage::list_issues` was building its SQL by appending the
label-membership filters BEFORE the per-status / per-priority /
per-timestamp predicates. SQLite's planner generally evaluates
AND-linked predicates left-to-right and pushes them into the scan
in source order. The label filter — which under multi-label AND
expands into a subquery against the labels table — is materially
more expensive per candidate row than the indexed status / priority
/ timestamp comparators. Putting it FIRST meant every candidate row
paid for the labels-table subquery before the cheap indexed
predicates had a chance to discard it.

Move the `append_label_membership_filters(...)` call AFTER the
`statuses`, `priorities`, `assignee`, `creator`, `not_blocked`,
`since`, and `until` predicate appends, in BOTH `list_issues` and
the parallel `count_issues_with_filters` shape. The query plan
now narrows the row set via the indexed predicates first and only
hits the labels subquery for survivors.

Same shape change as the recent search-filter reorder commit; the
SQL semantics are identical (AND is commutative) but evaluation
cost drops materially on large backlogs filtered by status +
labels — which is the dominant `br list --status=open --labels
foo,bar` shape.

No public-API change; both functions still bind exactly the same
parameter sequence in the same logical groups. Only the SQL string
order changes.
